---
title: "Alex Copeland"
format: 
  html:
    embed-resources: true
editor: visual
---

# Dataset

This dataset comes from tidytuesday: https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/blob/main/data/2022/2022-03-29/readme.md

# Introduction

In this study, there will be multiple research questions asked based on collegiate sports and their budgets. 


# Questions

1. Do public and private institutions differ in athletic revenue for selected sports?

2. Are there differences between the sports revenue?

3. Which sport generates the best profit?

# Methods

## Data Cleaning

The dataset was filtered to contain only NCAA Division I schools. This allows for more consistent budgets, since smaller schools usually have less funding. Also, the dataset was filtered to only contain the four sports basketball, football, baseball, and softball due to every other sport having very little budgeting. Two new variables sector and profit were created. Sector is the categorical variable that represents whether a schools is public or private. The variable profit was defined to be

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
$\text{Profit} = \text{Total Revenue - Total Expenditure}$ 
:::

## Research Question One
For research question one, the data were subset into public and private schools based off the variable sector. A t-test was conducted on mean total revenue between the two groups of schools. This test allows us to see whether the mean total revenue is different between public and private schools. Because of the data being heavily right skewed, two box plots were made. The first box plot only plots the observations with less than 12.5$m revenue to interpret the boxes easier. Note that this does not remove observations, instead it does not show them on the plot. For the second box plot, it plots the entire dataset.

## Research Questions Two and Three

For research questions two and three, both performed a Kruskal-Wallis test to compare distributions between variables. Research question two ask whether total revenue differs across all sports, and research question three asks if profit differs across all sports. Both questions violate the normality assumption the ANOVA test makes, this can also be seen in the histograms below. Because of this, both questions used the nonparametric alternative, the Kruskal-Wallis test.

Research question two and three overall were both signficant, so a post-hoc Dunn's test was run to find which specific pairwise comparison were signficantly different. For research question two and three all pairwise comparisons of sports revenue and sports profit were stastically different. 

# Analysis

```{R}
# Kruskal Wallis notes
# https://biostatsquid.com/comparing-multiple-groups-kruskal-wallis-test-in-r/
# https://library.virginia.edu/data/articles/getting-started-with-the-kruskal-wallis-test

# Breaking normailty assumptions
# https://www.datanovia.com/en/lessons/anova-in-r/


```


```{r}
#| echo: false

sports <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2022/2022-03-29/sports.csv', show_col_types = FALSE)
```

```{R}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false

library(ggplot2)
library(rstatix)
library(dunn.test)

# do kable and make tables pretty tables and summary statistics table
```

```{R}
#| echo: false

# Subset data into only Division 1 schools
d1 <- sports[sports$classification_name == "NCAA Division I-FBS" | sports$classification_name == "NCAA Division I-FCS" | sports$classification_name == "NCAA Division I without football", ]

# rename for graphing purposes
d1$Sports <- d1$sports

# Subset again on high revenue sports
d1_high_revenue <- d1[d1$Sports == "Basketball" | d1$Sports == "Baseball" | d1$Sports == "Football" | d1$Sports == "Softball", ]

# Define "empty" sector column
d1_high_revenue$Sector <- "Other"

# Add new column for Public or Private
d1_high_revenue$Sector[d1_high_revenue$sector_name == "Public, 4-year or above"] <- "Public"
d1_high_revenue$Sector[d1_high_revenue$sector_name == "Private nonprofit, 4-year or above" | d1_high_revenue$sector_name == "Private for-profit, 4-year or above"] <- "Private"
```

# Question One
Do public and private institutions differ in athletic revenue for high revenue sports?

```{R}
#| echo: false

# ---- Question One ----
# Do public and private institutions differ in athletic revenue for selected sports?

# Subset data into public and private schools
d1_public <- d1_high_revenue[d1_high_revenue$Sector == "Public", ]
d1_private <- d1_high_revenue[d1_high_revenue$Sector == "Private", ]

# sd
sd_public <- sd(d1_public$total_rev_menwomen, na.rm = TRUE)
sd_private <- sd(d1_private$total_rev_menwomen, na.rm = TRUE)

rq1 <- t.test(d1_public$total_rev_menwomen, d1_private$total_rev_menwomen)


```

An independent-samples t test was conducted to compare mean total revenue between public and private institutions. Public institutions had a mean total revenue of M = 7,341,701 (SD = 16473707), whereas private institutions had a mean of M = 4,862,737 (SD = 9706167). This difference was statistically significant, t(5560.8) = 7.24, p < .0001. These results indicate that public schools have significantly higher sports revenue than private schools.

The boxplot below also supports sports revenue differs between sectors.

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
$\textbf{Figure 1}$ 

```{R}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false

# coord cartesian (ylim) (in office hours)
# Change axis notation (https://scales.r-lib.org/reference/label_number.html)

# Trimed Plot
rq1_plot_trim <- ggplot(d1_high_revenue, aes(x = Sector, y = total_rev_menwomen, fill = Sector)) + geom_boxplot() + labs(title = "Total Revenue by College Sector (Trimmed to fit)", x = "Sector", y = "Revenue") + theme_bw() + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(scale = 1e-6, suffix = "M"), breaks = c(0,2000000, 4000000, 6000000, 8000000, 10000000, 12000000)) + coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0,12500000))

rq1_plot_trim
```
:::

This is the boxplot showing all observations. Note that the prior boxplot did not remove the observation, instead it did not show them on the graph.

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
$\textbf{Figure 2}$

```{R}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false

# Change axis notation (https://scales.r-lib.org/reference/label_number.html)
rq1_plot <- ggplot(d1_high_revenue, aes(x = Sector, y = total_rev_menwomen, fill = Sector)) + geom_boxplot() + labs(title = "Total Revenue by College Sector", x = "Sector", y = "Revenue") + theme_bw() + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(scale = 1e-6, suffix = "M"))

rq1_plot
```
:::





# Question Two

Are there differences between the sports revenue?

```{R}
#| echo: false

# ---- Question Two ----
# Are there differences between the sports revenue?

# non parametric compared to anova (kruskal wallis test)
# Kruskal assigns ranks smallest to largest to the entire revenue column, then test on the average rank per sport. 

rq2 <- kruskal.test(total_rev_menwomen ~ Sports, data = d1_high_revenue)


# Dunns table says column mean - row mean. so if (r1, c1) > 0, then column has a higher revenue. If (r1, c1) < 0, then column has a lower revenue. Confirm findings with boxplot to see the order.

dunn_result <- dunn.test(d1_high_revenue$total_rev_menwomen, d1_high_revenue$Sports)

#n = 6105
rq2_n <-kruskal_effsize(d1_high_revenue, total_rev_menwomen ~ Sports)

```

A Kruskal-Wallis test was conducted to examine differences in total revenue for each college sport. The overall test was statistically significant, $\chi^2$(3) = 4266.08, p < 0.0001. Based on this, it can be concluded that there is  statistical evidence of differences in total revenue for each of the sports. Additionally, it was found that the effect size was large $\eta^2$(0.70), indicating that sports explains a large role in the differences of total revenue. 

A post-hoc Dunn's test was run to determine which sports revenue were different. The test showed that each pairwise comparison of revenue were statistically different. The box plot below also supports these findings.

::: {style="text-align: center; padding: 20px 0;"}
$\textbf{Figure 3}$

```{R}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false

rq2_plot <- ggplot(d1_high_revenue, aes(x = Sports, y = total_rev_menwomen, fill = sports)) + geom_bar(stat = "summary", fun = "mean", color = "black") + labs(title = "Mean Total Revenue by Sport", x = "Sport", y = "Mean Total Revenue") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(scale = 1e-6, suffix = "M"), breaks = (c(0, 1000000, 5000000, 10000000, 15000000 ,20000000, 25000000, 30000000, 35000000))) + theme_bw() + geom_hline(yintercept = 19924123.7, linetype = "dotted") + geom_hline(yintercept = 1164631.9, linetype = "dotted") + geom_hline(yintercept = 6490734.6, linetype = "dotted") + geom_hline(yintercept = 709709.2, linetype = "dotted")

rq2_plot
```





















# Question Three
Which sport generates the best profit?

```{R}
#| echo: false

# ---- Question Three ----
# Which sport generates the best profit?

# Create profit column
d1_high_revenue$profit <- d1_high_revenue$total_rev_menwomen - d1_high_revenue$total_exp_menwomen



rq3 <- kruskal.test(profit ~ Sports, data = d1_high_revenue)
rq3_n <- kruskal_effsize(d1_high_revenue, profit ~ Sports)

# Dunns table says column mean - row mean. so if (r1, c1) > 0, then column has a higher revenue. If (r1, c1) < 0, then column has a lower revenue. Confirm findings with boxplot to see the order.

rq3_dunn <- dunn.test(d1_high_revenue$profit, d1_high_revenue$Sports)
```

A Kruskal-Wallis test was conducted to examine differences in profits for each college sport. The overall test was statistically significant, $\chi^2$(3) = 620.45, p < 0.0001. Based on this, it can be concluded that there is  statistical evidence of differences in profits for each of the sports. Additionally, it was found that the effect size was moderate $\eta^2$(0.10), indicating that sports explains a moderate role in the differences of profits for each sport. 

A post-hoc Dunn's test was run to determine which sports profit were different. The test showed that all pairwise comparisons of profit were statistically different. The box plot below also supports these findings. Notice that 


```{R}
#| echo: false

# Change from scientific notation in df, (https://www.r-bloggers.com/2022/08/how-to-turn-off-scientific-notation-in-r/)
options(scipen = 999)


# Stacked bar plot with y axis
rq3_plot <- ggplot(d1_high_revenue, aes(x = Sports, y = profit, fill = Sports)) + geom_bar(position = "stack", stat = "summary", fun = "mean", color = "black") + labs(title = "Mean Total Profit by Sport", x = "Sport", y = "Profit") +  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_number(scale = 1e-6, suffix = "M"), breaks = (c(-300000, 0, 1000000, 3000000, 5000000, 7000000))) + theme_bw() + geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "solid")

rq3_plot

```
